titleStmt.title: Translation of BL Cotton Ch. xviii.30

p.seg: The agent of the prior and community of St Andrews cathedral church says and argues in law before you, Lord Abbot of Balmerino, the only judge assigned by the Apostolic See, against the master, prioress, and the nuns of Haddington, that although they themselves – the prior and community – are the true rectors of the church of Haddington, the master of the nuns of Haddington, the prioress, and nuns aforesaid, have appropriated for some time certain teinds within the said parish of Haddington belonging to the prior and community by common right, namely, the teind of one ploughgate of land in the territory of Athelstaneford, which once belonged to the territory of Drem, and the teinds of two oxgangs of land contained in the old garden of Stevenson, and the teinds of the land that lies between the old garden and the toun of Haddington, and the teinds of half an acre of land between the mill of Stevenson and the teinds of the crofts of certain burgessses of Haddington, and the small teinds of Byres, and of the cottaries of Byres and of Barney, and they have turned them over to their own use to the no-small detriment and damage of the prior and community as well as the grave injury of their monastery, and until now have not been afraid to appropriate [them]; wherefore the agent of the aforesaid prior and convent seeks the aforesaid teinds as the right and property of his aforesaid church of Haddingotn, and thus by you to be declared to belong to the monastery of St Andrews, not withstanding the letters, instruments, judgments, penalties, renunciations, and confirmations obtained from the Apostolic See in the common form, interposed perchance through whichsoever prior or priors of the monastery of St Andrews; and the aforesaid master, prioress, and nuns be compelled by you to cease from the appropriation of the said teinds in future by definitive judicial sentence; he also seeks the said master, prioress, and nuns to be condemned in the aforesaid teinds received in the meantime which they assess as a hundred pounds sterling, and the condemned to be compelled to pay; and he also seeks costs incurred for entry of the lawsuit, which he assesses as ten pounds sterling; and he bears witness in respect of the things to be done in the legal proceedings for the place and time of asking, invoking your service in all things in which it was invoked by law, not constraining yourself to a categorical proving of all things in general and particular, but only so far as he may obtain so much as he may have proved in the foregoing.
